Peel the backing from the template and stick it temporarily to the surface for accurate alignment. 3. **Attach the mount plate to the T-rail clips**: Align the mount plate holes (marked with a "G") with the T-rail clips and secure them using the provided hardware. 4. **Install the clips onto the T-rail**: Rotate each clip and snap it firmly into place on the T-rail — ensure the black foam pads compress slightly upon installation for secure grip and vibration damping. Note: For recessed T-rail installations, additional modifications may be required (e.g., cable routing through the guard). Always route the Ethernet cable from an active 802.3af PoE switch or injector, observing the 100 m (300 ft) maximum PoE cable length limit.

为相机供电的步骤如下： 1. 确保电池已充电（出厂时电池未充满，首次使用前请先充电）； 2. 将充满电的电池正确装入相机电池仓，并关闭仓盖； 3. 顺时针转动镜头环（即电源开关）即可开机 <PIC>； 4. 开机后相机将显示拍摄画面 <PIC>。 另可选供电方式： • 使用AC适配器套件ACK-E2（选配）和DC耦合器，连接家用电源插座供电（需先将DC耦合器插入相机底部接口并固定到位）<PIC>。 , ["Manual29_17", "Manual29_35", "Manual29_17"]

The proper way to use the brake lever and parking brake button is as follows: 1. **To engage the parking brake**: Squeeze the brake lever $\textcircled{1}$, then push down the parking brake button $\circledcirc$ while slowly releasing the brake lever — this locks the brake in place. 2. **To release the parking brake**: Simply squeeze the brake lever again — the parking brake will disengage automatically. ⚠️ **Important Safety Notes**: - Always apply the parking brake before starting the engine. - Never operate the machine with the parking brake engaged — it may overheat the brake disc and reduce braking performance. - Ensure the brake lever free play is within specification: 6–7 mm (0.24–0.28 in). <PIC> - Confirm the brake lever end does not extend beyond the handlebar end to prevent damage during service. <PIC> , ["brake_lever_free_play", "brake_lever_position"]

Before using a snowmobile, perform the following essential pre-operation checks: 1. **Read the Owner's Manual and all safety labels** — Understand controls, warnings, and emergency procedures. 2. **Fuel system**: Ensure fuel tank is filled with appropriate fuel and the cap is securely closed. 3. **Engine oil level**: Check and top up if necessary (per maintenance schedule). 4. **Throttle lever**: Verify smooth, full-range operation and proper return to idle. 5. **Brake system**: Test brake lever for firmness, responsiveness, and proper parking brake engagement/release. 6. **Steering system**: Confirm free, smooth movement of handlebars and ski alignment. 7. **Drive belt & guard**: Inspect drive belt for cracks, fraying, or improper tension; ensure drive guard is in place and secure. 8. **Drive track**: Check for proper tension, damage (cuts, missing lugs), and correct alignment. 9. **Ski/ski runners & slide runners**: Examine for wear, damage, or lack of lubrication; ensure skis are aligned. 10. **Lights**: Test headlight, brake light, and any other required lighting (especially for low-visibility conditions). 11. **Battery**: Confirm charge level and terminal connections are clean and tight. 12. **Fittings & fasteners**: Inspect for loose bolts, nuts, or damaged components (e.g., shroud latches, suspension mounts). 13. **Parking brake**: Set before starting engine; never operate with it engaged. 14. **Drive select lever position**: Confirm it’s in Neutral (or correct starting position) and throttle is fully released before moving. 15. **Personal protective equipment (PPE)**: Wear an approved helmet, eye protection (goggles/face shield), insulated snowmobile suit, boots, and gloves that allow full control operation. Also: Avoid operating indoors; ensure adequate ventilation when starting; inspect trail conditions; ride only on snow/ice — never on dirt, pavement, or glare ice without extreme caution.

To start the engine of your snowmobile, follow these detailed steps based on the official manual: 1. **Perform Pre-Operation Checks** (see page 6-1): Confirm fuel level is adequate, engine oil is at proper level, throttle lever moves freely, brake functions correctly, V-belt and drive track are intact, ski/runners are aligned, lights work, battery is charged, and all fasteners are secure. 2. **Set the Parking Brake**: Always engage the parking brake before attempting to start the engine — this is a mandatory safety requirement (see WARNING in Ref. 4 & 6). 3. **Check Control Positions**: Ensure the drive select lever (if equipped) is in Neutral or correct starting position (e.g., 'Forward' or 'Low', depending on model), and the throttle lever is fully released. 4. **Prepare Engine for Starting**: - If cold: Use the **starter lever (choke)** as instructed (Ref. 1, p. 5-1); - If equipped with a primer pump: Press the **primer pump knob** several times (Ref. 1, p. 5-2); - Confirm fuel cock lever is in the 'ON' position (Ref. 1, p. 5-10). 5. **Start the Engine**: - Turn the main switch to 'ON'; - Press or pull the starter mechanism (electric or recoil/manual starter — see Ref. 1, p. 7-1 and p. 6-4); - Once started, let the engine idle and warm up before releasing the parking brake. 6. **Safety Reminders**: - Never start or run the engine indoors without proper ventilation (Ref. 3); - Do not operate with parking brake applied (Ref. 4 & 6); - Always wear protective gear including an approved helmet and eye protection (Ref. 4 & 6). For model-specific details (e.g., BR250T, ET410TR, VK540E), refer to sections 'STARTING THE ENGINE' (p. 7-1) and 'EMERGENCY ENGINE STARTING' (pp. 7-3–7-4) in your Owner’s Manual.

A V-Belt Holder is a dedicated mounting component designed to securely store and hold a spare V-belt for emergency use. It ensures the spare belt remains properly positioned—away from heat sources (e.g., hot muffler) and moving parts—and ready for quick replacement. When installing a V-belt into the holder, always tighten the securing bolt (①) and confirm the belt is seated firmly to prevent loosening, damage, or safety hazards.

Adjusting the throttle cable on a snowmobile involves the following steps: 1. **Ensure engine idle speed is properly adjusted first** — this is a prerequisite for accurate throttle cable adjustment. 2. **Loosen the adjuster lock nut** (typically located near the carburetor or handlebar assembly). 3. **Turn the adjuster in or out** until the correct throttle lever free play is achieved — usually 1–3 mm (0.04–0.12 in), as specified in your model’s manual. # CAUTION: - Always verify that the throttle outer cable is firmly seated in its holder after adjustment. - Confirm the throttle operates smoothly and returns fully to idle position when released. - Never allow the adjusting bolt to move while loosening or tightening the lock nut. For safety and precision, perform this adjustment with the snowmobile on level ground, engine off, and parking brake engaged.

The proper way to use the steering system on a snowmobile relies primarily on coordinated body movement ('body English') combined with handlebar input. As you approach a turn, slow down and gently turn the handlebars in the desired direction. At the same time, shift your weight onto the inside running board and lean your upper body into the turn — the sharper or faster the turn, the more you should lean. This technique must be practiced at low speeds in a safe, flat, obstacle-free area before attempting higher speeds or tighter turns. Avoid abrupt steering, rapid throttle changes, or excessive braking during turns, as these can cause loss of traction, tipping, or uncontrolled spins — especially on ice, hard-packed snow, or glare ice where steering response is reduced. Always keep both hands on the handlebars and maintain proper riding posture for optimal control.

The correct steps to start and operate a snowmobile are as follows: 1. Before starting, ensure the engine is properly warmed up and perform all pre-operation checks (e.g., track tension, brake function, fuel level). 2. Set the parking brake, then start the engine following the instructions in your Owner's Manual. 3. Once idling, release the parking brake by squeezing the brake lever. 4. Select the desired gear position using the shift lever: “FWD” (Forward), “REV” (Reverse), or “L” (Low-speed Forward) — ensure throttle is fully released and machine is at full stop before shifting. 5. Apply the throttle slowly and smoothly to begin acceleration; the centrifugal clutch will engage automatically. 6. To turn: slow down before the curve, turn the handlebars in the desired direction, lean your upper body into the turn, and put your weight on the inside running board. 7. To stop: release the throttle first, then gently apply the brake — avoid sudden braking, especially on ice or hard-packed snow. 8. Always keep both hands on the handlebars, wear proper protective clothing (helmet, goggles, snowmobile suit), and ride only on snow or ice surfaces. 9. For safety, avoid high speeds until fully familiar with handling, and never operate on dirt, sand, rocks, grass, or pavement.

Proper techniques for riding uphill on a snowmobile include: 1. Practice first on gentle slopes and progress to steeper climbs only after building skill. 2. Accelerate before starting the climb, then reduce throttle opening to prevent track slippage. 3. Keep your weight on the uphill side at all times — lean forward when climbing straight up; on steeper inclines, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars. 4. Slow down as you approach the crest and be prepared for obstacles, drops, or other people/vehicles on the far side. 5. If unable to continue, stop the engine, set the parking brake, pivot the rear of the snowmobile to point downhill (never stand on the downhill side), then restart, release the brake, and descend carefully. Note: Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginners.

Proper techniques for riding downhill on a snowmobile include: 1. Keep speed to a minimum — avoid high speeds until fully familiar with your machine. 2. Apply just enough throttle to keep the centrifugal clutch engaged; this allows engine compression to assist in slowing the machine and prevents free rolling. 3. Use the brake frequently with light, controlled pressure — never apply excessive braking, as it may lock the track and cause loss of control. 4. Maintain proper body position: stay centered or slightly forward, keep both hands on the handlebars, and be ready to react to obstacles, drops, or other trail users. 5. Always ride on appropriate surfaces (snow or ice only); avoid glare ice, hard-packed snow, or mixed-dirt/sand snow, as these reduce traction and increase stopping distance. 6. Remember that many surfaces require longer stopping distances — begin decelerating early and plan ahead. For beginners, steep or side-hill descents are not recommended.

Correct techniques for crossing a slope (side hill) on a snowmobile include: 1. Proper weight positioning — lean your body toward the uphill side to maintain balance; 2. Recommended riding posture — kneel with the downhill knee on the seat and the uphill foot on the running board, allowing easy weight shifting; 3. Be prepared for sideways slipping — if slippage occurs, steer *in the direction of the slide* (if no obstacles are present), then gradually correct back to your intended path; 4. If tipping begins, steer *down the hill* to regain balance; 5. **Critical warning**: Side hills are not recommended for beginners or novice riders; 6. If balance cannot be recovered and tipping is imminent, dismount *immediately on the uphill side*. Always ride cautiously on slopes — avoid excessive speed, abrupt throttle changes, or hard braking, especially on icy or hard-packed snow surfaces.

Inspecting the spark plug on a snowmobile involves the following steps, based on the official maintenance guidance: 1. **Ensure safety**: Turn off the engine and allow it to cool completely. Set the parking brake and disconnect the spark plug wire(s) to prevent accidental starting. 2. **Locate the spark plug(s)**: Refer to your snowmobile’s manual — the spark plug holder is listed in the controls section (Section 5-10), and access typically requires removing engine room plates (Sections 5-8 to 5-9). 3. **Remove the spark plug**: Use a proper spark plug socket and ratchet. Avoid cross-threading; loosen carefully. 4. **Inspect the condition**: Examine the white porcelain insulator around the center electrode. The ideal color is medium to light tan for normal operation. Abnormal colors indicate potential issues: - Very white: possible intake air leak or carburetion problem; - Black or oily: fouling due to rich mixture or oil ingress; - Eroded or damaged electrodes: wear from heat/deposits. ⚠️ Do not attempt self-diagnosis of engine faults — consult an authorized dealer if abnormalities are found. 5. **Check electrode gap**: Before reinstalling (or if replacing), measure the gap using a wire thickness gauge and adjust to the manufacturer’s specification <PIC>. 6. **Verify thread reach**: Ensure the new or reinstalled spark plug has the correct thread length (reach). Too long → overheating/engine damage; too short → fouling, hot spots, or thread damage. 7. **Reinstall securely**: Hand-thread first, then tighten to the specified torque (not provided in reference docs — consult dealer or service manual). ⚠️ Always use the spark plug type and specifications recommended by the manufacturer. Never substitute without dealer approval. , ["drill0_07"]

The provided reference documents do not contain specific step-by-step instructions for cleaning a snowmobile. However, based on standard snowmobile maintenance best practices and the limited mention in Reference Document 7 ('4. Clean around the fuel-tank cap.'), general safe cleaning steps include: 1. Allow the engine and exhaust system to cool completely before cleaning. 2. Remove surface snow and slush with a soft brush or cloth — avoid high-pressure water near electrical components, bearings, or the engine bay. 3. Gently clean the fuel tank cap area (as noted) to prevent debris from entering the tank. 4. Wipe down plastic, painted, and chrome surfaces with a mild soap-and-water solution and a microfiber cloth. 5. Lubricate ski runners and slide rails with appropriate wax or silicone-based protectant after drying. 6. Check and clean air intake vents and cooling fins to ensure proper airflow. For detailed, model-specific cleaning procedures, please consult your snowmobile’s official Owner’s Manual or contact an authorized dealer.

The best methods for memorizing communication channels using a manual program are as follows: 1. **Using the MEMORY/ERASE button on the remote control**: - Press the △/▽ or NUMBER buttons to select the desired channel number. - Press the MEMORY/ERASE button to choose 'Memory' — an on-screen display will confirm the action. 2. **Using the MENU system**: - Press MENU, then use △/▽ to navigate to the main menu. - Press ▶ and select 'Manual'. - Press ▶ again and select 'Channel'. - Press ▶ and use △/▽ to select the channel number you wish to memorize or erase. Note: MANUAL PROGRAM is ideal for adding or deleting specific channels after initial AUTO PROGRAM has been run. For best results, ensure signal quality is strong — if poor, re-run AUTO PROGRAM first. Also, if wireless and cable channels overlap, use number buttons (0–9) to switch between them.

Common causes and conditions that can lead to poor television or radio signal reception include: 1. **Weak signal area (fringe reception)**: Being located at the edge of a broadcast coverage zone results in low signal strength, often causing snow-like dots on TV screens or weak audio on radio. 2. **Signal reflection (ghosting)**: Occurs when the signal splits—traveling via both a direct path and a reflected path off buildings, hills, or other obstacles—causing overlapping or shadowed images. 3. **Electromagnetic interference**: Sources such as automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electrical drills, aircraft, or passing cars emit strong random signals that disrupt reception, leading to fluttering, drifting, black spots, or horizontal streaking. 4. **Antenna-related issues**: Using an indoor antenna instead of an outdoor one, or having a severely weathered, damaged, or improperly installed antenna/cable (e.g., corroded connectors, degraded 300-ohm flat wire or coaxial cable) significantly reduces signal quality. 5. **Poor antenna positioning**: Incorrect direction or placement fails to optimize line-of-sight with the broadcast tower; adjusting antenna orientation or height may improve reception. 6. **Weak or unstable antenna signal input**: As noted, 'the signal from the antenna is weak' directly contributes to poor reception — this may stem from distance, obstructions (e.g., mountains, dense foliage), or inadequate antenna gain.

To enable and customize captions and on-screen text settings on your television, follow these general steps (note: exact menu names and navigation may vary slightly by model): 1. Press the MENU button on your remote control or TV to open the main on-screen menu. 2. Navigate using the △/▽ and ▶ buttons to locate and select 'Caption', 'Closed Caption', or 'Accessibility' (some models label it under 'Settings' > 'System' > 'Captions'). 3. Press ▶ or ENTER to enter the caption submenu. 4. Select 'On' or 'CC1' (or similar option) to enable closed captions. 5. To customize appearance (font size, color, background, style), look for options like 'Caption Style', 'Text Settings', or 'Caption Appearance' — if available on your model — and adjust using △/▽ and ▶ buttons. 6. Press MENU repeatedly or press EXIT/BACK to save and exit. Note: Closed captioning is supported on some models only, and real-time or prerecorded program captions must be broadcast with embedded caption data. For on-screen language (e.g., OSD menus), go to 'Language' in the main menu (as described in Reference Doc 4).

To properly connect and set up an outdoor antenna for optimal signal reception, follow these key steps: 1. **Inspect the antenna and cable**: Before installation, check that the outdoor antenna and coaxial/flat wire cables are not severely weathered or damaged — deterioration can significantly reduce signal quality. 2. **Choose the right cable type and adapter**: - For a **300 ohm flat wire**: • Connect it to the screws on a 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter. • Then push the adapter’s 75 ohm end into the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack. - For a **75 ohm coaxial cable**: • Connect it directly to the 75 ohm antenna jack — no adapter needed. 3. **Optimize placement**: Mount the antenna as high and unobstructed as possible (e.g., roof or mast), oriented toward broadcast towers. If you’re in a weak or fringe signal area (e.g., rural or mountainous zones), a directional or amplified antenna may be necessary for reliable reception <PIC>. 4. **Verify signal strength**: After connection, perform a channel scan on your TV. Note that the TV remembers the last input mode used, even after power-off. For best results, consult a local service center for antenna selection advice tailored to your location and signal environment. , ["Manual18_9"]

To properly connect a DVD player to a television or audio system for optimal performance, follow these steps: 1. **Choose the best available video connection** (in order of priority): - Use **HDMI** (if both devices support it) for full HD/4K video + digital audio in one cable — *not explicitly mentioned in reference docs but industry standard and optimal*. - If HDMI is unavailable: Use **Component (Y/Pb/Pr)** cables for 480i/720p analog HD video — as noted in Doc 1: "Connect component DVD inputs to Y, PB, PR (480i) and audio IN to audio (L/R) ports." - Next option: **S-Video** (for better quality than composite) — per Doc 4: "Connect the S-VHS output jack of the VCR to the S-VIDEO jack on the TV." - Last resort: **Composite (R/Y/W or yellow/red/white)** — implied in Docs 3 & 5 (e.g., "audio/video output jacks" to "IN 1(A/V) jacks"). 2. **Connect audio separately if not using HDMI**: - For stereo sound: Connect DVD’s **AUDIO L/R (red/white)** outputs to TV or audio system’s corresponding **AUDIO IN (L/R)** — per Docs 1, 3, and 5. - For mono compatibility: If using a mono source, connect to **AUDIO L/MONO IN**, not just R — per Doc 4. 3. **Select the correct input mode on the TV**: - After connecting, turn on both devices. - Press the **TV/VIDEO button** (on remote or front panel) to cycle through inputs — per Docs 2 and 5. - Select **VIDEO 1**, **VIDEO 2**, **S-VIDEO**, or **COMPONENT**, depending on which jacks you used — as specified in Docs 2, 3, 4, and 5. - Note: The TV remembers the last selected mode even after power-off — per Docs 1 and 5. 4. **Optimize audio/video settings** (optional but recommended): - For audio: Use the **MTS button** to select MONO/STEREO/SAP if broadcasting supports it (Doc 6); note MTS does *not* operate in video mode. - For sound tuning: Access **Auto Sound Control** via MENU → DASP → choose preset (e.g., Movie, Music) — per Doc 6. ⚠️ Important notes: - Always match color-coded cables (e.g., yellow→video, red→audio right, white→audio left) — per Doc 7’s COLOR CODE section. - Front-panel inputs take priority over rear-panel ones if both are connected simultaneously — per Doc 4. - Ensure all connections are secure and avoid placing devices near strong magnetic sources (e.g., large speakers) to preserve picture quality — per Doc 7’s Installation section.

When troubleshooting technical issues or malfunctions, the key factors to check include: 1. **Observe warning indicators** — such as displayed messages (e.g., 'Check Engine', 'Low Oil Pressure', 'Over Temperature') and audible alerts (buzzer); 2. **Verify power and connections** — ensure proper AC power supply, correct voltage setting, and secure/unplugged cables before handling components; 3. **Inspect physical conditions** — check for leaks (e.g., coolant, oil, bilge water), loose fasteners, damaged cables, or foreign objects near circuitry; 4. **Review environmental factors** — avoid dust, moisture, extreme temperatures, or electrical interference (e.g., shared circuits with high-power appliances); 5. **Consult the Troubleshooting chart** — cross-reference 'TROUBLE', 'POSSIBLE CAUSE', 'REMEDY', and 'PAGE' entries in the manual for structured diagnosis; 6. **Confirm operational history** — note any recent changes, modifications, or distinct performance shifts that may indicate underlying faults. If the cause remains unclear or repair procedures are not covered, contact an authorized service provider. , ["Manual31_17"]

To ensure safe operation, please follow these essential safety precautions: 1. **General Electrical Safety**: Read all instructions before use; do not operate outdoors or near water/flame; keep power cord away from edges, heat sources, and children; always unplug by grasping the plug—not the cord; never use extension cords or adapters; ensure grounded outlet only. 2. **Thermal & Pressure Hazards**: Do not touch hot surfaces (e.g., lid, inner pot, muffler, engine); use heat-resistant gloves/hand protection when handling hot parts; never open the appliance while pressurized—confirm float valve is down and no resistance when opening; avoid placing face/hands over steam release valve or air vents during/after operation. 3. **Mechanical & Operational Safety**: Keep body and clothing away from rotating/moving parts; do not operate with guards, shields, or shroud removed, open, or unlatched; never carry passengers or mow in reverse without looking behind; avoid slopes >15°; do not use near drop-offs, on unstable ground, or in poor visibility/lightning conditions. 4. **Maintenance & Inspection**: Before each use, check steam release valve, pipe, anti-block shield, and float valve for clogs; inspect sealing ring, heating element, and cord for damage; ensure inner pot is dry and debris-free before insertion; never modify, disassemble, or repair the appliance yourself. 5. **Usage Limits**: Do not overfill (max 2/3 for general cooking, 1/2 for expanding foods); do not deep fry or pressure fry with oil; do not use unauthorized accessories, pots, or attachments; do not operate if damaged, malfunctioning, or dropped. 6. **Supervision & Environment**: Not for commercial use; children ≥8 years may use only under direct supervision; keep appliance on stable, level, non-combustible surface; allow full cooling before cleaning/storage. <PIC> For full details—including slope operation, assembly warnings, and magnetic safety—refer to your Operator’s Manual and safety labels. , ["Manual14_0"]

To adjust the intensity settings on your electric toothbrush to suit your oral care needs: 1. **Manual adjustment**: Press the intensity indicator lights on the handle (located near the power button) to cycle through the 3 intensity levels: - Low intensity (one light illuminated) - Medium intensity (two lights illuminated) - High intensity (three lights illuminated) 2. **Timing flexibility**: You can change the intensity before, during, or after brushing — no need to restart. 3. **Adaptive Intensity feature**: Your toothbrush automatically lowers intensity by one level if excess pressure is applied for an extended time, helping protect your gums. After each new brushing cycle, it returns to your pre-selected setting. 4. **Personalization via app**: While no mode/intensity is displayed on the handle, you can fully customize and save your preferred intensity (and other settings) using the companion app — see 'App - Getting Started' for setup instructions. Note: All intensity settings are safe for use with braces, fillings, crowns, and veneers.

To activate or deactivate features on your electric toothbrush for customized usage, follow these steps: 【From the handle】 - Step 1: Place the handle on the charging stand. - Step 2: Press and hold the power button for a specific duration (exact timing not specified in documentation; refer to your product’s quick start guide or app for precise instructions). - Intensity settings can be manually selected at any time (before, during, or after brushing) by pressing the intensity indicator lights on the handle to cycle through Low (1 light), Medium (2 lights), and High (3 lights). 【From the app】 All major customizable features — including Adaptive Intensity, Scrubbing Feedback, Pressure Sensor Feedback (note: Pressure Sensor is activated by default and can be deactivated via app), and Brush head replacement reminder — can be enabled or disabled via the companion app. To use the app: 1. Download the official app and ensure Bluetooth is enabled on your smartphone. 2. Pick up the toothbrush to activate it (lights will illuminate). 3. Open the app and follow the guided setup steps to pair your toothbrush. 4. Create your account and complete any firmware update if prompted. 5. Navigate to 'App – Features' or 'Settings' to toggle individual features on/off. Important notes: - Adaptive Intensity is activated by default and automatically lowers intensity if excessive pressure is detected; it deactivates when Pressure Sensor is turned off. - Scrubbing Feedback is deactivated by default; when enabled, the brushing feedback light (amber) illuminates and vibration changes to remind you to stop scrubbing. - Brush head replacement reminder is activated by default. - The SenseIQ indicator (on handle) illuminates when smart features are active — during brushing or when enabling/disabling settings. - Although modes aren’t displayed on the handle, all mode selections (e.g., Clean, Whitening, Gum Care) are stored in the app and applied automatically upon next use.

To charge your electric toothbrush inside the travel case, follow these steps: 1. Plug the USB-C cord into the travel case and connect the other end to the USB wall adapter. 2. Plug the wall adapter into an electrical outlet. 3. Place the toothbrush handle into the travel case — ensure it is properly seated on the internal charging contacts. 4. Leave the travel case plugged in until fully charged (up to 16 hours). The battery indicator light will blink during charging and turn off (stop blinking) when fully charged <PIC>. For proper charging, make sure the travel case is placed on its side for stability <PIC>, and ensure both the toothbrush handle and brush head are completely dry before placing them inside <PIC>. , ["toothbrush0_05", "toothbrush0_06", "toothbrush0_07"]
